parents.father_research_notes: Ensign Levi Andrews of Newington/New Britain CT
parents.mother_research_notes: Chloe, dau. of Robert Welles of Newington

sources.short: Camp's History of New Britain CT

notable.notable_blurb: Latin lexicographer behind Harper's Latin Dictionary

narrative_blocks.header: His Latin dictionary became Harper's Latin Dictionary
narrative_blocks.body: Andrews published a Latin-English lexicon in 1850 that was revised into Harper's Latin Dictionary, a standard reference well into the twentieth century. Yale awarded him a doctorate in 1848 for his classical scholarship.

narrative_blocks.header: He taught ancient languages across the country
narrative_blocks.body: A Yale graduate of 1810, Andrews practiced law before turning to teaching, holding a professorship of ancient languages at the University of North Carolina and later running schools in New Haven and Boston. He co-wrote a widely used Latin grammar.

narrative_blocks.header: He sat as legislator, magistrate, and judge
narrative_blocks.body: Back in New Britain, Andrews served as a Whig state legislator, a magistrate, and a judge of probate. He kept at his books to the end, dying in 1858 in the midst of his literary labors.

narrative_blocks.header: His book on slavery drew abolitionist fire
narrative_blocks.body: In 1836 Andrews published a study of slavery and the domestic slave trade that The Liberator attacked as a pro-slavery apologia. The work set the scholar awkwardly against the rising antislavery tide of his native Connecticut.

education.institution_blurb: Graduated Yale 1810; Yale conferred LL.D. 1848 for Latin scholarship.
Yale conferred LL.D. 1848 for eminence as Latin author.

narrative_blocks.header: He taught English literature at Alabama
narrative_blocks.body: A graduate of the University of North Carolina in 1823, Sims studied two years at the University of Halle in Germany before becoming a professor of English literature at the University of Alabama. He had earlier led an academy at La Grange, Alabama.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: The Gothic Revival house he built and named for his first wife, Patience Barrow.

narrative_blocks.body: An honor graduate of Franklin College, McKinley studied law under Judge Joseph Henry Lumpkin and won election to the Georgia legislature from Oglethorpe County in 1835. He returned to the legislature during Reconstruction at his friends' urging.

narrative_blocks.header: They called him the legislature's Chesterfield
narrative_blocks.body: McKinley was remembered for the courtesy of his manner and his wise, conservative counsel, earning the nickname the Chesterfield of the legislature. A devout Presbyterian elder, he was known as a man of broad sympathies.

narrative_blocks.header: He built Barrowville from brick he kilned himself
narrative_blocks.body: A devoted experimental farmer, McKinley built his Gothic-style house Barrowville in 1858 and 1859 from brick fired in his own yard, adapting a design from Andrew Jackson Downing's pattern book. He kept detailed records of his plantation's agriculture and weather.

narrative_blocks.header: Sherman's army camped around his house
narrative_blocks.body: During Sherman's March to the Sea, Barrowville was made the headquarters of a Federal encampment, with thousands of soldiers camped around it. The house narrowly escaped burning when the last troops marched away.

narrative_blocks.header: He died at twenty-one of fever
narrative_blocks.body: Andrew McKinley, known in the family as Arny, died in 1875 at twenty-one after a two-week bout of fever. He was buried with military honors by his company, the Baldwin Blues.

narrative_blocks.header: His son Lee lived past a hundred
narrative_blocks.body: Guy married twice, to Mary Goetchius and then to Forrest Lee Carrington, raising children of both marriages near Milledgeville. His son Lee Carrington McKinley lived from 1898 to 2001, past the age of a hundred.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Hooker' honors the Hooker family descent through grandmother Lucina Hooker Cowles (H00309).

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.
